summary:
Aung San Suu Kyi emerged as Myanmar's (Burma's) foremost pro-democracy figure following the military crackdown on the 1988 popular uprising against the socialist government of General Ne Win. The daughter of Burma's independence hero Aung San, she co-founded the National League for Democracy and became the symbol of peaceful resistance against military rule. The military junta placed her under house arrest in 1989 in an attempt to sideline her before the 1990 elections.

The NLD won approximately 80 percent of parliamentary seats in those elections — a result the military refused to honor and never implemented. Aung San Suu Kyi remained under house arrest for most of the period from 1989 to 2010, a total of nearly 15 years. She was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1991, which she could not collect in person; her sons accepted it on her behalf. Her prolonged detention drew sustained international attention and made Myanmar's military government a pariah in international opinion.

Her non-violent approach, dignified bearing under confinement, and explicit invocation of Buddhist ethics made her an iconic figure in the global human rights movement through the 1990s. Her final release from house arrest in 2010 was followed by electoral participation, and the NLD won elections in 2015, bringing her to de facto leadership. Her international reputation was later severely damaged when she failed to speak out against military atrocities against the Rohingya Muslim minority, illustrating the complex compromises faced by political leaders emerging from long periods of opposition.
